fuzzy_prd:
We need a reusable toolkit that lets developers define a closed set of named values once and then interact with them throughout an application without scattering raw literals everywhere. Each entry in a set should have a symbolic identifier, a stored value, and a human-readable display text. When only a value is given, the display text should be derived automatically from the identifier. Display texts must support per-language overrides so the same set can be presented in multiple locales without duplicating data.

The toolkit should expose the full set in several formats: an ordered list of values, a count, a minimum-to-maximum range, the full key-value-label breakdown, and a widget-ready list of label-value pairs. It should also allow lookups in both directions (identifier-to-value and value-to-identifier) returning a neutral absent marker rather than crashing on misses.

Beyond the set itself, the toolkit should let a developer bind one of a host object's fields to such a set. Once bound, the field should automatically gain: a human description of its current value, optional per-entry boolean state helpers, optional per-entry mutation helpers, optional per-entry query-scope helpers (only when the host supports scoping), optional strategy-object dispatch, and validation rules. Validation defaults to an inclusion check allowing blank; a presence rule is added only when explicitly requested; all validation can be suppressed. Look at the helper-generation and scope-creation sections of the binding module to understand the prefix convention and how strategy classes are located by title-casing the entry identifier.

hidden_constraints.description: Constant identifier derivation from entry key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The constant identifier is derived by upcasing the entry key and replacing every dash or contiguous whitespace run with a single underscore. The stored value is preserved verbatim, including any embedded capitalization, dashes, or spaces. For example, the key 'pt-BR' yields constant 'PT_BR' with value 'pt-BR'.

hidden_constraints.description: Default label fallback when no explicit label supplied
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an entry is declared with only a value and no explicit label, the human label defaults to a title-cased rendering of the entry key: underscores are replaced with spaces and each word is capitalized. For example, key 'value_one' yields label 'Value One'.

hidden_constraints.description: Ordering strategy: sort by name (entry key), value, or translation (label)
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The value set supports four ordering strategies: declaration order (default or explicit 'none'), sort by entry key ('name'), sort by stored value ('value'), and sort by human label ('translation'). Ordering affects all set-level outputs including the values list, label-value pairs, and JSON serialization. The 'sort_by' strategy is set at definition time.

All lookup operations return a neutral absent marker (rendered as 'nil' in plain-text output) rather than raising an error when nothing matches. This applies to: lookups by constant identifier, by entry key (including a null key), by value, and to the attribute humanize method when the attribute holds no value.

hidden_constraints.description: Validation rules: default inclusion, optional presence, skip flag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, associating an attribute registers an inclusion rule restricting the attribute to the set's values with allow_blank=true. A presence rule is registered only when the 'required' option is passed; if 'required' carries extra options (e.g. a conditional), those are forwarded to the presence rule. Setting the skip flag suppresses all validation rules entirely, producing no registrations.

hidden_constraints.description: State helpers disabled when create_helpers is false
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When helper generation is disabled (create_helpers: false or not requested), no per-entry boolean state-check methods or mutator methods exist on the host object. Querying such a method returns the absent marker 'nil', not false.

hidden_constraints.description: Query scopes not generated when host does not respond to scope
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Query scope helpers are generated only when the host class responds to a scope-registration facility. If the host does not support scoping, declaring the association with scope-creation enabled produces no filters and raises no error. The output in that case is 'filters=none'.

hidden_constraints.description: Strategy object class located by title-casing entry key; absent when attribute unset
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The strategy-object accessor resolves the attribute's current value to a live instance of a class whose name is the title-cased form of the matching entry's key (e.g. key 'normal' -> class 'Normal', key 'crazy' -> class 'Crazy'). An optional suffix overrides the accessor name only, not the class lookup. When the attribute is unset (nil), the accessor returns nil, rendered as 'object=nil'.

hidden_constraints.description: List-form definition: token becomes both key and value; label is title-cased key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the value set is declared in 'list' form, each token in the list becomes both the entry's key and its stored value (verbatim). The human label defaults to the title-cased rendering of the token. Constants are derived by upcasing the token and replacing dashes/whitespace with underscores.

hidden_constraints.description: Locale fallback: nonexistent locale yields title-cased labels
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the active locale has no override table defined at all (e.g. locale 'inexistent'), all labels fall back to the title-cased rendering of the entry key, regardless of whether other locales have tables. This applies to JSON serialization, labeled-value pairs, and single-value translation.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: helper-generation and scope-creation sections of the binding module
context_pointers.explanation: The ClassMethods module defines has_enumeration_for, which accepts :create_helpers and :create_scopes options. The create_helper_methods method generates per-entry boolean predicates; create_mutator_methods generates bang mutators; create_polymorphic_methods generates the strategy-object accessor; create_scopes generates named query filters. The prefix convention is controlled by passing a Hash with key :prefix to these options.

context_pointers.vague_reference: prefix convention and how strategy classes are located by title-casing the entry identifier
context_pointers.explanation: Strategy class lookup: klass.const_get(klass.key_for(value).to_s.camelize).new — the entry key is title-cased (camelized) to form the strategy class name. The accessor suffix defaults to '_object' and can be overridden via helpers[:polymorphic][:suffix].

EnumerateIt is a module that extends a host class via extend EnumerateIt, which injects ClassMethods including has_enumeration_for. The host gains enumerations class attribute and all the helper-generation methods.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail is not specified in the product requirements. Please check the source files under golden/lib/enumerate_it/ for the authoritative behavior, or consult the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for concrete input/output examples.

feature1_constants.json.description: Define a set of named entries and read back the auto-generated public constants. From a definition that maps each entry's symbolic key to a value (optionally paired with a human label) — or from a bare list of raw tokens — the toolkit exposes one accessible constant per entry whose value is the entry's value. The constant identifier is derived from the entry key by upcasing it and replacing every dash or whitespace run with an underscore, while the value itself is preserved verbatim (including embedded capitalization, dashes, or spaces).

feature2_list.json.description: Return the ordered collection of all values defined in the enumeration. By default the values are listed in the order the entries were declared. A definition may also request a default ordering strategy: order by value, by entry key, by human label, or explicitly none (declaration order). The output is the list of values, one per line, in the resolved order.

feature3_enumeration.json.description: Return the full internal specification of the enumeration: for every entry, its symbolic key together with the value and human label associated with it. Entries are reported in declaration order, one line per entry, exposing the key, the value, and the label.

cases.expected_output:
key=value_1 value=1 label=Hey, I am 1!
key=value_2 value=2 label=Hey, I am 2!
key=value_3 value=3 label=Hey, I am 3!

feature4_length.json.description: Report how many entries the enumeration contains. The output is the count of defined entries.

feature5_to_a.json.description: Produce a list of human-label / value pairs suitable for populating selection widgets. For each entry, the pair is the entry's human label followed by its value. When an entry was declared with only a value and no explicit label, the label defaults to a title-cased rendering of the entry key. The pairs follow the enumeration's resolved ordering (declaration order by default, or the requested ordering strategy: by value, by key, by label, or none). One pair is emitted per line.

cases.expected_output:
label=Hey, I am 1! value=1
label=Hey, I am 2! value=2
label=Hey, I am 3! value=3

label=abc value=3
label=fgh value=2
label=jkl value=0
label=xyz value=1

feature6_to_a_translation.json.description: Produce the human-label / value pairs while honoring a localization table. For entries declared without an explicit label, the human label is resolved against a per-locale override table keyed by entry key under the active locale; entries with no matching override fall back to a title-cased rendering of the key. Switching the active locale changes the resolved labels accordingly. One pair is emitted per line.

cases.expected_output:
label=First Value value=1
label=Value Two value=2

label=Primeiro Valor value=1
label=Value Two value=2

feature7_to_json.json.description: Serialize the enumeration to a JSON array of objects, one per entry in declaration order, where each object carries a value field and a label field. The label honors the active-locale override table when present and otherwise falls back to a title-cased rendering of the entry key; when the active locale has no override table at all, every label uses the title-cased fallback. The output is the JSON text.

feature8_translate_value.json.description: Given a single value, return the human label associated with that value, resolved against the active-locale override table. The input names the value to translate; the output is the resolved label for the matching entry.

feature9_range.json.description: Return an inclusive interval spanning the enumeration's values, from the smallest value to the largest. The output reports the lower and upper bounds of that interval.

feature10_labels.json.description: Return the complete list of human labels for the enumeration, in declaration order, one label per line. Each label is the entry's human label (or the title-cased fallback derived from the key when none was supplied).

cases.expected_output:
Hey, I am 1!
Hey, I am 2!
Hey, I am 3!

feature11_values_by_constant_names.json.description: Given a list of constant identifiers, resolve each to the value of the matching entry, preserving the requested order. An identifier that does not correspond to any defined constant resolves to an absent marker rather than raising. One resolution is emitted per line.

feature12_value_by_constant_name.json.description: Given a single constant identifier, return the value of the matching entry. When no constant matches the supplied identifier, the result is an absent marker rather than an error.

feature13_value_by_key.json.description: Given an entry key, return the value of that entry. A key that is not part of the enumeration yields an absent marker, and so does an absent (null) key. The lookup accepts the key in its plain textual form.

feature14_keys.json.description: Return the list of entry keys used to define the enumeration, in declaration order, one key per line.

feature15_key_by_value.json.description: Given a value, return the entry key that maps to it. A value that is not present in the enumeration yields an absent marker.

feature16_attribute_label.json.description: Associate one of a host object's attributes with an enumeration and derive a human-readable description of the attribute's current value. Given the attribute's current value, the description is the human label of the matching entry (the entry's supplied label, or a title-cased fallback derived from the key when none was supplied). When the attribute holds no value, the description is absent.

feature17_attribute_label_translation.js.description: Derive the human-readable description of an associated attribute's value while honoring localization. For entries whose label is resolved through the active-locale override table, the attribute description follows the locale: the same value yields different descriptions under different active locales.

feature18_state_checks.json.description: Optionally generate per-entry boolean state-check helpers on the host object that report whether the associated attribute currently equals a given entry's value. When helper generation is enabled, one check exists per entry and returns true only for the entry matching the attribute's current value. An optional prefix derived from the attribute name may be prepended to each check. When helper generation is not requested, no such checks exist on the object. The output reports, per entry, the check's answer.

feature19_state_setter.json.description: Optionally generate per-entry mutator helpers on the host object that set the associated attribute to a chosen entry's value. Invoking the mutator for a target entry overwrites the attribute with that entry's value. An optional prefix derived from the attribute name may be prepended to each mutator. The input names the target entry key to switch to; the output reports the attribute's value after the mutation.

feature20_strategy_object.json.description: Associate an attribute with an enumeration whose entries each have a matching strategy object, and resolve the attribute's current value to a live strategy instance. The enumeration has two entries: one keyed `normal` and one keyed `crazy`. Each entry has a corresponding strategy class (named by title-casing the key) exposing a single operation that decorates a supplied message: the `normal` strategy returns the message prefixed with "I'm Normal: ", and the `crazy` strategy returns it prefixed with "Whoa!: ". The accessor returns a fresh strategy instance for the attribute's current value, or an absent marker when the attribute is unset. An optional suffix customizes the accessor name only. The output reports the resolved strategy's class name and the result of invoking its operation with the supplied message.

feature21_query_filters.json.description: When the host class supports a query-scoping facility, optionally generate one named query filter per enumeration entry; each filter, when invoked, builds a query that constrains the associated attribute to that entry's value. An optional prefix derived from the attribute name may be prepended to each filter name. If the host class does not support query scoping, declaring the association generates no filters and raises no error. The output lists the generated filter names and, when exercised, the attribute/value constraint each filter builds.

cases.expected_output:
filter=value_1
filter=value_2
filter=value_3
query filter=value_1 attribute=foobar value=1
query filter=value_2 attribute=foobar value=2
query filter=value_3 attribute=foobar value=3

feature22_validation_rules.json.description: When the host class exposes validation facilities, associating an attribute with an enumeration registers validation rules. By default an inclusion rule is registered restricting the attribute to the enumeration's values and permitting blank. A presence rule is registered only when presence is required; if presence is required with extra options (such as a conditional), those options are carried into the presence rule. Requesting that validation be skipped suppresses both rules. The output lists each registered rule with its attribute and pertinent parameters.
